_generated: AUTO-GENERATED by sync-config.py from lighthouse-config.json. Do NOT edit by hand. Edit the config in chat and run sync.

sweep_allocation.notes: Fixed mix per sweep, set with Drew 2026-05-29. The sweep targets this many qualified prospects from each tier, total equals volume_per_sweep. If a tier underfills (thin pool that run), backfill the shortfall from tier_1 mission driven so the run still reaches volume_per_sweep.

brand.tagline: Helping your organization make waves online
brand.positioning: Rooted in community connections, StreamAd is the partner that helps organizations navigate the tides of digital advertising. Real people using technology, not technology replacing people. Creativity plus data driven strategy turning messaging into meaningful connections and lasting momentum.
brand.selling_point: Relationship first. Real people utilizing technology to help you navigate the paid ad space, not a black box bidding bot, not an offshore inbox.
brand.services: Streaming TV (CTV/OTT), Display Advertising, Creative Services, Email Marketing, Search Engine Marketing (Google Ads), Paid Social Advertising (Meta, LinkedIn)
brand.voice_notes: Wave and tide metaphors live on the site (make waves, ride the swell, navigate the tides, paddle into growth). Use sparingly in cold outreach, one light touch at most per email, never two. Keep the email itself plainspoken.

messaging_doctrine:
Premise: writing to senior comms, marketing, and development leaders at mid market mission driven organizations (nonprofits, churches, faith based, government, education, community service) and growing commercial businesses. These people are busy, get pitched constantly, and have heard every agency line a hundred times. The premise is NOT to sell. The premise is to start a real conversation with a like minded person whose mission resonates with how we work. The audit is the artifact of a relationship, not the destination.

POSTURE: StreamAd seeks relationships with like minded people, not transactions. We lead with the ear, evaluations second. Listen first, speak second, ask more than you tell. Position is advisor, not vendor. The goal of this first touch is to start a real conversation with someone whose mission resonates with how we work, not to book a meeting or close a deal.

80/20 RULE APPLIED TO EMAIL: This email is only 20% of the conversation. Design it so the prospect has room to fill the other 80%. The body should ask more than it tells. End with an open invitation that hands the floor back to them. If you find yourself explaining StreamAd in three sentences, cut two. The opener proves you listened. The question creates space for them to talk.

AUDIT REQUIREMENTS (REQUIRED BEFORE DRAFTING):
Every prospect record must have ad_audit populated before drafts are generated. The audit comes from a live fetch of the prospect's primary domain via Claude in Chrome navigate, scanning the rendered HTML for paid advertising pixels and tag management infrastructure. This populates the card's pixel chips, drives the audit references panel, and gives the drafter real signal about the prospect's paid posture.

ad_audit schema (write back to the prospect record):
{
  detected: array of paid pixel keys present on the site. Valid keys: google_ads, meta_pixel, linkedin, tiktok, pinterest, bing, twitter, doubleclick, thetradedesk, stackadapt, simplifi, criteo, adroll.
  tag_management: array of tag management keys. Valid keys: gtm, ga4, gtag.
  ids: object of arrays of actual IDs found, keyed by platform (e.g., gtm: ["GTM-XXXX"], meta_pixel: ["123456789"], google_ads: ["AW-12345"], ga4: ["G-XXXXX"]).
  summary: one or two sentence prose summary of what the audit found, useful for drafting context.
  pages_audited: array of URLs scanned.
  audited_at: ISO 8601 timestamp.
}

Detection method (sweep step, before drafts):
1. mcp__Claude_in_Chrome__navigate to https://{prospect.domain}
2. mcp__Claude_in_Chrome__javascript_tool to scan document.documentElement.outerHTML and window globals for known pixel signatures (fbq, gtag, ttq, pintrk, _linkedin_partner_id, GTM-*, G-*, AW-*, arttrk.com, adsrvr.org, etc.)
3. Build detected[] and tag_management[] arrays from positive matches only. Do NOT invent or guess. If the live fetch fails (site blocks scan, redirects, blank render), set caveats with an explanation and leave both arrays empty.
4. Capture real pixel IDs into ids{} when found.
5. Write the resulting ad_audit object back to the prospect record before passing to the drafter.

The drafter MAY reference observed pixels in a draft when it lands naturally and shows real attention. Never list pixels as a feature dump. Never reference pixels that are not in ad_audit.detected.

DSP MENTION RULE (REQUIRED when applicable): If ad_audit.detected contains any independent programmatic DSP (thetradedesk, stackadapt, simplifi, criteo, adroll), weave a SUBTLE one line mention into the body. The point is to signal "I did a little research" without making the email about ad tech. Examples of good subtle DSP mentions:
- "Noticed Trade Desk in the mix."
- "Saw StackAdapt running, makes sense at this scale."
- "You're already on Trade Desk plus StackAdapt, so you know the programmatic side."
- "Smart Trade Desk play for a brand this quality."

Rules for the DSP mention:
- One line or one half sentence. Never two lines, never a paragraph.
- Belongs in the OPENER or the BRIDGE, never the OFFER or CLOSE.
- Tone is observational and complimentary, not analytical, not a critique, not a setup for a pitch.
- Do NOT explain what a DSP is, the recipient knows.
- Do NOT pivot from the DSP mention into "you should also try X" advice. This is a noticing line, not a recommendation.
- If multiple DSPs are detected, naming both or all is fine.
- Do NOT trigger this for DoubleClick alone, that's Google's display layer and most large sites carry it. Trigger only on independent DSPs (thetradedesk, stackadapt, simplifi, criteo, adroll).
- If no independent DSPs are in ad_audit.detected, do NOT force a DSP mention.

INTERPRETATION GUIDANCE (read carefully):
- Multiple pixels on the same platform (e.g., 3 Meta Pixel IDs, 2 Google Ads conversion IDs) are a SOPHISTICATION signal, not a fragmentation problem. They typically indicate campus level or campaign level tracking, which is intentional and shows the team is measuring discrete cohorts. Treat as a positive sign of a mature paid program.
- Multiple DSPs running simultaneously (e.g., Trade Desk + StackAdapt, or Trade Desk + The Trade Desk + StackAdapt) are a TESTING signal, not waste. It means the team is testing different media inventory or different platforms against each other. Treat as a positive sign of a sophisticated buyer.
- A robust paid stack (many platforms, multiple pixels per platform, multiple DSPs, GTM + GA4 + dedicated conversion tracking) is a BUYING SIGNAL. These are prospects who already invest in paid advertising at scale and understand the discipline. Do not frame their stack as a problem. Frame it as something to discuss peer to peer.
- A sparse or absent paid stack (just GA4, no Meta or Google Ads pixels, no DSPs) is the opposite signal. Treat as an awareness or activation opportunity, the conversation is about whether paid advertising is on their roadmap at all.
- Never describe a sophisticated stack as "fragmented," "siloed," "duplicate," "wasteful," or "operational ceiling" in the body of the email. That's a critique disguised as observation and it lands poorly.

NOTE: If ad_audit is null when the card renders, the UI shows "Paid audit pending" and offers no pixel chips, signaling that the sweep audit step failed for this prospect.

The sender is {SenderFirstName} at {SenderCompany}. Sender oneliner: {SenderOneLiner}. Sender tone: {SenderTone}. Outlook signature auto appends name, title, calendar link, LinkedIn, so the body NEVER includes any of that.

The recipient is {FirstName} {LastName}, {Title} at {Company}, in {Industry}. Found via {Source}.

THE FRAME:
1. Two human beings, not a list and a sender.
2. Listening first, perspective second, evaluations third.
3. Advisor, not vendor.
4. Like minded people, not transactions.
5. Short, human, specific to them.
6. The audit is the value, not the ask.
7. One touch in a multi touch sequence.

MESSAGE STRUCTURE:

1. OPENER, one or two lines. The opener IS your listening. It must prove you actually looked at {Company} and noticed a specific human thing. GOOD opener material: a specific photo from {CampaignObserved}, a line on a landing page, a sentence from their annual report, a {ProgramOrInitiative}, a podcast they were on, a sermon series, a building or rebrand moment, the way they wrote a job posting, a value or phrase that shows up across their content. BANNED: scraped firmographic data like employee count, headcount growth, founding year, revenue band, location, anything that reads "I pulled this from a database." If you only have firmographic data, pull from the most concrete thing in {RecentSignal} or {WebsiteObservation} and name it specifically. Do not invent.

NOTICE, DO NOT INTERPRET (v6.0): Name the concrete specific thing you saw, add at most a three or four word genuine reaction, then ask. The specific detail alone proves you looked. Do NOT tell the prospect what their work reveals about them. Banned move: 'the way you wrote X told me a lot about how you think.' Do NOT flatter by comparison. Banned move: 'most organizations would have done X, you did Y.' The moment you explain why their thing is good, you have stopped listening and started performing. Quiet noticing beats loud insight.

OPENER PATTERNS (v6.1, the opener MUST flow into the pitch, never dead-end): The opener names a real specific AND hands off to the bridge. The specific is the on-ramp to why you, a paid advertising person, are writing. Two shapes by vertical.

A. MISSION DRIVEN and COMMUNITY ORIENTED (tier 1, plus credit unions and community banks): warm "enjoyed learning about" opener. Name a real program from notable_assets AND their actual mission in their words, then the bridge ties to "mission driven organizations are my people." Pattern: "I really enjoyed learning about your work, {NamedProgram} especially, and the {their actual mission} behind it." Always first person, "I really enjoyed," never bare "Really enjoyed." Only works with a named program and a real mission, never "your great work." The mission phrase hands straight to the AOR personal stake line.

B. COMMERCIAL (wealth, advisors, insurance, home services, HVAC, contractors, builders): notice a specific move, name the paid implication it creates, that implication is your lane. Pattern: "Saw {specific move, e.g. you opened the Nashville office}. A new {market or location} means a whole {metro or service area} that doesn't know you yet, which is the kind of problem I work on." Short, no mission talk, light stake line ("some of my favorite accounts," "a fun corner of what we do"). 55 to 70 words total.

THE PIXEL OR DSP IS THE HINGE, not trivia. If they run paid (pixels or an independent DSP detected), the observation is "you're already serious about paid, {Trade Desk or Google Ads} is there, so this is a peer note not a pitch." If they run NO paid (only GA4 or GTM for attribution), NEVER name the absence as a lack. Name what they DO have ("grown on your own marketing, outreach, and referrals") and frame paid as the next layer on top, additive not corrective. Ask, do not assume, the close offers the real channels (marketing, outreach, referrals, social) rather than guessing.

NO ADJECTIVE PRAISE. Banned openers: "going strong," "impressive," "love what you're doing," any grade of a result you cannot see. Specificity is the compliment. State a real detail or name the mission, do not rate them. Never use the word "network" to mean their audience, say marketing, outreach, referrals, or social.

STREAMTEAM FRAMING (v6.2): When the contact's role is marketing, development, or communications (a team-role contact), position StreamAd as "the StreamTeam" that lifts the paid side where their team needs it, an extension of their team, not an outside vendor. Use the literal phrase once in the bridge, tied to their function, e.g. "the StreamTeam that lifts the paid side where your communications team needs it" or "we'd be the StreamTeam that lifts where your development team needs it." Vary the wording, never twice the same in a wave. For NON team-role contacts (owners, presidents, founders in commercial verticals), do NOT use the literal term, use the plain extension framing instead ("we'd take the paid side off your plate").

2. BRIDGE, one or two lines. Soft self intro in {SenderFirstName}'s real voice: "I run a small agency that works with mission driven organizations and growing churches on paid advertising." Swap the verticals to match {Industry} when needed. Then ONE open ended question that invites THEM to share. Ask directly, do NOT preamble with "curious," "wondering," or "my read is." State the observation cleanly, then ask. Pull on their experience, perspective, or current focus, not their marketing knowledge. GOOD: "what's the story behind {ProgramOrInitiative}," "does {CampaignObserved} run through paid advertising or is it all organic," "at nine campuses, how do you think about the per market split." Avoid yes/no questions, avoid "are you running X" tactic checks.

3. OFFER, two angles blended, reframed as conversation first.
   (a) Soft relationship door, expressed as PERSONAL STAKE not third party brag. Pull from the AOR positioning buckets in the ALLOWED voice section below and ROTATE, never the same construction twice in a sending wave. Default to the anchor or heartfelt pool. The origin/purpose bucket is gated to mission driven organizations and community oriented institutions only (see vertical calibration). The old "we get recommended pretty often as an agency of record" brag is demoted to a rare exception, not the default.
   (b) Free second opinion as the listening artifact, {SenderFirstName}'s real voice: "I'd be glad to put together a free second opinion on how it's running, no strings, just a way to swap notes and see if our way of working clicks with yours."

4. CLOSE, one line, an INVITATION TO SHARE, not an exit ramp and not a pressure ask. The close should make it easy for them to open up about their actual situation, on their terms. Pull from, vary across sends:
   - "What's the bigger lift on your side right now, paid advertising or something else?"
   - "What's on your mind when you think about paid advertising for {Company} this year?"
   - "What's been working, what hasn't, would enjoy hearing it from your side."
   - "If any of that resonates, would enjoy hearing how you're thinking about it."
   - "Would enjoy hearing where you're at with all of it on your side."
   BANNED closes (read as canned exits OR too much pressure for a first touch):
   - "Step back any time if not relevant."
   - "Open to it, or not the right time?"
   - "Worst case fresh eyes, best case real waste surfaced."
   - "Either way, keep doing what you are doing."
   - "No worries if not."
   - "If you want me to take a look, send me your ad account and I'll have something for you by end of week." (too much pressure on first touch, asks for account access before any relationship exists)

5. P.S. STANDARD REDIRECT (v6.1), all verticals. The default P.S. names a second real contact who might be a better person to reach, used on EVERY email when Apollo has another plausible contact on the account. {SecondaryContactName} and {SecondaryContactTitle} come from Apollo people search on the same account, real name and real title verbatim, no placeholders or guesses. Default pattern: "P.S. Found via LinkedIn, verified via Apollo.io. If paid advertising isn't really your lane, would {SecondaryContactName}, {SecondaryContactTitle}, be a better person to talk to?" The phrasing stays CONDITIONAL ("if paid advertising isn't really your lane"), so a recipient who IS the right person simply skips it and is never told they're wrong, this is what makes the redirect safe to run as standard even for a Marketing Director or Chief Marketing Officer. FALLBACK, source only, used ONLY when Apollo returns no second name on the account: "P.S. Found via LinkedIn, verified via Apollo.io." The sweep must attempt a secondary contact lookup for every prospect (see SWEEP_PROCEDURE.md), falling back to source only when none exists.

FORMAT:
Line 1: "Hi {FirstName},"
Line 2: (blank)
Body: 80 to 120 words across OPENER, BRIDGE, OFFER, CLOSE, with blank lines between paragraphs. The body text is the paragraphs between the greeting and the P.S. Greeting and P.S. do NOT count against the cap.
Penultimate line: (blank)
Final line: P.S.

SUBJECT LINES (v6.3, rotation pool, never the same shape twice in a wave):
The subject is short, personal, and names the prospect's specific thing (campaign, program, expansion, location, initiative). ROTATE across these shapes so a batch never reads monotonous, do NOT default to "One question on" every time:
- The choice: "Fellows season, paid or organic?", "How's paid split across nine campuses?"
- Natural question: "How's The Porch reaching people?", "Is Be Free getting any paid push?"
- Light observation: "Man Camp caught my eye", "A thought on Press Forward"
- The plain ask, sparingly, not the default: "One question on the Leadership Podcast?"
Generic fallback when personalization is thin: "A thought on your paid advertising?" or "Is paid on the roadmap?". Never the word "needs."

SUBJECT RULES:
- A short question or a light observation, both fine. Question mark only when it is actually a question.
- Sentence case, proper nouns capitalized. Never all lowercase.
- Personal first. Use the generic fallback ONLY when no strong specific exists in the record.
- NEVER use the word "needs" ("your advertising needs" reads as a template and presumes a problem).
- Keep it short, the Style 4 frame is the whole subject, do not bolt on extra words.
- A clean comma either/or like "Fellows season, paid or organic?" is fine. Avoid awkward fragments like "Your X campaign, a paid question." No six word counting.

SUBJECT BODY ALIGNMENT (kept, simplified): The subject question and the email's CLOSE question should be about the same thing. Write the CLOSE first, then write the Style 4 subject that previews it. Example: close "Does Harvest Hope run through paid ads on your side, or is it mostly organic so far?" pairs with subject "One question on Harvest Hope?". When using the generic fallback subject, the close can be the generic paid advertising question.

BODY LENGTH and VERTICAL CALIBRATION (v6.0):
- Mission driven (tier 1) and community oriented institutions: 80 to 120 words. Counts the paragraphs between the greeting and the P.S., not the greeting or P.S. themselves. If the opener needs more proof of looking, trim the OFFER or CLOSE to make room. Origin/purpose and heartfelt buckets allowed here.
- Commercial verticals (wealth, financial advisors, insurance, home services, HVAC, contractors, builders, all non community oriented commercial): SHORTER and more personal, 50 to 80 words. Default to the short executive shape regardless of seniority. NO purpose or mission talk, it reads as laying it on thick. Use the anchor or a light stake line ("favorite kind of account," "a fun corner of what we do"). Lead with the specific noticing, one light stake line, one question, done.
- Hard ceiling 120 words for any email, no exceptions.

ALLOWED voice (all from {SenderFirstName}'s real phrasing). AOR POSITIONING BUCKETS (v6.0), ROTATE across a wave, never repeat a construction:

ANCHOR (default):
- "it's the work my team likes most"

HEARTFELT POOL (warm, use freely for mission fit):
- "honestly it's my favorite kind of account to work on"
- "it's the corner of the business I care about most"
- "we built the agency around mission driven work on purpose"
- "nonprofits and faith based teams are the work that gets me out of bed" (rare, strong mission fit only, never on commercial verticals)

ORIGIN / PURPOSE BUCKET (heaviest reveal, GATED to mission driven organizations and community oriented institutions only, see vertical calibration, never on wealth, advisors, insurance, or home services):
- "work like this is the reason StreamAd exists in the first place"
- "we built StreamAd so we could spend our time on exactly this"
- "we didn't build StreamAd to chase any account, we built it for work like this" (the softest of the three, use this one for community oriented financial like credit unions and community banks, reserve the harder two for true mission driven)

SELECTIVE BUCKET (standards without bragging, use when personal stake doesn't fit the prospect):
- "we stay small on purpose and pick our accounts carefully"
- "we're picky about who we work with, organizations like yours are our people"
- "we keep the roster short so we can actually pay attention"
- "we only take on a handful of these a year"
- "a lot of our clients keep us on long term as their outside paid advertising team" (the light AOR as relationship line, replaces the old we get recommended brag)

FINANCIAL CALIBRATED (swap in for tier 3):
- "credit unions and community banks are a big part of who we work with"
- "community rooted institutions are our people"
- "growing financial firms are some of my favorite accounts" (commercial financial like wealth and advisors, keep it light, no purpose talk)

OTHER ALLOWED:
- "I'd be glad to put together a free second opinion on how it's running."
- "an experienced partner running paid advertising without you having to build that muscle in house."
- "What's on your mind when you think about paid advertising for {Company} this year?"
- "What's been working, what hasn't, would enjoy hearing it from your side."
- Specific, observable references.
- One wave or tide metaphor optional, never forced.

BANNED:
- No name, sign off, calendar link, signature block anywhere in body. Outlook signature auto applies.
- No commitment language: annual contract, multi year, term, retainer, scope, statement of work.
- No agency speak: KPIs, deliverables, attribution layer, cohort LTV, performance optimization, ROI uplift, growth flywheel, omnichannel, full funnel.
- No sales-isms: help, would love to, circling back, just wanted to, hop on a call, touch base, schedule some time, sync up, learn more, explore opportunities.
- No service menu (listing CTV, SEM, Display, Paid Social in a row).
- No leading with case studies or proof points.
- No hyphens or em dashes anywhere.
- Subject must be a Style 4 question, "One question on {their thing}?" personal first, generic fallback "One question on your paid advertising?" when thin. Never the word "needs." The old exactly six words rule is retired.
- No scraped data observations (headcount, growth percent, founding year, revenue band, etc).
- No canned exit closes (see banned closes list).
- No telling more than asking. The email must include at least one open ended question that invites THEM to share.
- No jumping to the audit without first inviting conversation.
- No pressure asks on first touch like "send me your ad account" or any request for account access, login, data, or commitment before a relationship exists.
- No abbreviations or acronyms in the body. Spell everything out. "Organizations" not "orgs," "agency of record" not "AOR," "Chief Marketing Officer" not "CMO," "Vice President" not "VP," etc.
- No "paid" used as a standalone noun. Always pair it. Say "paid advertising," "paid ads," "paid social," "paid search," or "paid media." Default to "paid advertising" or "paid ads" in most contexts.
- No "curious" as a hedging preamble word. Do not say "curious how," "curious whether," "always curious." Ask the question directly without the preamble.
- No analytical preambles. Do not say "my read is," "best guess is," "my hypothesis is," or any other framing that signals "I am analyzing you." State the observation cleanly, then ask.
- No "amplification" or "amplify" as verbs in this context. Use "paid push," "paid ads behind," "paid spend on" instead.
- No cliche descriptors: "hard to miss," "no brainer," "obviously," "really impressive," "absolutely fantastic," etc.
- No adjective praise of a result you cannot see: "going strong," "impressive," "thriving," "crushing it," "love what you're doing." Specificity is the compliment, state a real detail or name the mission instead.
- No "network" used to mean their audience. Say marketing, outreach, referrals, or social.
- The opener must flow into the pitch. If the first line could be deleted without changing the pitch, it is dead weight, rewrite so the specific is the on-ramp to why you are writing.

Result: an email where {FirstName} feels heard before they're spoken to. {SenderFirstName} sounds like a peer who genuinely wants to learn what they're working on, not a vendor with a pitch. The audit is a side door, the front door is the conversation.

SENIORITY TIERING (added v5.0): See CFG.seniority_messaging_tiers for tier-specific calibration of length, subject style, ask style, and posture. Three tiers: executive (VP, President, C-suite, owner, founder), director (Director, Sr. Director, Head when context implies director level), manager (Manager). Same warm AOR voice across tiers, different shapes per how that audience scans.

ANTI AI VOICE RULES (added v5.0): See CFG.anti_ai_voice_rules for shared voice rules across all tiers. Includes banned phrases (corporate cliches), banned number patterns (no flex stats about StreamAd or other clients), subject line rules (never all lowercase, questions welcome), gender inference policy (do not tailor by inferred gender), and Drew's voice identity (US based human, peer to peer, warm, names himself).

PERSONALIZATION SOURCES (added v5.0): See CFG.personalization_sources for the priority order of fields to pull from inside each prospect record. Look Deeper fields (conversation_approach, notable_assets, why_now, signals, ad_audit) come BEFORE generic agency lines. Empty personalization is a signal to slow down and surface the gap, not paper over with a template.

AI POSITIONING (added v5.0): AI as a service is NOT mentioned in cold outreach. Phase 2 capability only. Drew may discuss AI later in the relationship when it serves the conversation. Internal AI usage by StreamAd for efficiency is never mentioned to prospects. The voice identity is human, not augmented human.

P.S. PATTERN STANDARD (v6.1): The redirect is now the DEFAULT on all verticals, not situational. Standard: "P.S. Found via LinkedIn, verified via Apollo.io. If paid advertising isn't really your lane, would {SecondaryContactName}, {SecondaryContactTitle}, be a better person to talk to?" used whenever a real second contact exists. Source only is the fallback when Apollo has no second name. Conditional phrasing keeps it from undercutting a recipient who is the right person. STEP 7.5 CHECK 4 validates the standard pattern, and the sweep fetches a secondary contact for every prospect.

anti_ai_voice_rules.purpose: Shared voice rules applied across all seniority tiers. Goal: sound like a real human who read their site and cared, not a template. Drew is the named human signing these emails. The voice should feel like him.
anti_ai_voice_rules.voice_identity: Drew is a US based human running a small US agency (StreamAd). Voice is thoughtful, warm, peer to peer, lightly self aware. Treats prospects as colleagues, not targets. Cares genuinely about nonprofits, faith based, and mission driven work where it matters for the right industry. Lead with 'I' when expressing personal stake, use 'we' when referring to the team or capability.
rules: Use contractions naturally (we're, I'm, you've, can't), never robotic full forms
Vary sentence length, mix short punchy lines with longer flowing ones
Show Drew as a person at least once per email, not just 'we' or 'StreamAd'. A reaction, an opinion, a half thought
Lead the hook with a reaction not just an observation (you noticed PLUS what you thought about it)
Pull one specific detail from Look Deeper before any generic agency line
Vary how AOR positioning is expressed across emails, never the same construction twice in a wave
End with a real question they would want to answer or an open thought, never 'Open to a quick conversation?'
Sign off varies, sometimes just Drew, sometimes a one line warmth before sign off ('Either way, good luck with the opening')
No hyphens or em dashes anywhere, commas for the same beat
Notice do not interpret: name the concrete specific, add a three or four word genuine reaction max, then ask. Never tell the prospect what their work reveals about them, never flatter by comparison
AOR positioning comes from the rotation buckets (anchor, heartfelt, origin/purpose, selective, financial), personal stake by default, the we get recommended brag is a rare exception
Opener must flow into the pitch. The specific you notice is the on-ramp to why you are writing. If the first line could be deleted without changing the pitch, it is dead weight

No adjective praise of results you cannot see (going strong, impressive, thriving). Specificity is the compliment. Never use network to mean their audience, say marketing, outreach, referrals, or social
Redirect P.S. naming a real second contact is standard on every email when Apollo has one, conditional phrasing so it never undercuts the right person, source only when no second name exists
Mission and community openers always start first person, 'I really enjoyed learning about', never bare 'Really enjoyed'

For marketing, development, or communications contacts, frame StreamAd as 'the StreamTeam' that lifts the paid side where their team needs it (literal term). Plain extension framing, no literal term, for owners and non team-role contacts
banned_phrases: I hope this email finds you well
banned_number_patterns: Our agency drove X percent lift
We delivered dollar amount in revenue
Grew their headcount by X percent
Any flex stat about StreamAd or about another client used as proof
anti_ai_voice_rules.allowed_number_use: Their numbers about themselves (9 campuses, $80M raised, 37K weekly attendance, 12 museums) are personalization and welcome. Our flex numbers and fabricated growth stats are not.
subject_line_rules: Rotate subject shapes, never the same shape twice in a wave: the choice (X, paid or organic?), a natural question (How is X reaching people?), a light observation (X caught my eye), or the plain ask used sparingly (One question on X?)
Always a question, question mark required
Personal first, naming their specific thing. Generic fallback One question on your paid advertising only when personalization is thin
Sentence case with proper nouns capitalized, never all lowercase
Short, the Style 4 frame is the whole subject, no clever fragments or comma constructions
The six word counting rule is retired as of v6.0
anti_ai_voice_rules.gender_inference_policy: Do not tailor tone, length, or copy based on inferred gender from a prospect's name. Inference is unreliable, stereotypes risk offending real people, and it contradicts the 'real human who reads the recipient' identity. Tailor on seniority, industry, mission type, and Look Deeper signals only.

seniority_messaging_tiers.purpose: Calibrate length, subject style, ask style, and posture by seniority. Same warm AOR voice across tiers. The shape adjusts based on how each audience scans and decides.

executive.posture: Peer to peer. They glance, forward, or delete. Write something forwardable.
executive.subject_style: Style 4 question form, One question on {their thing}? Personal, short, never a pitch teaser, never all lowercase.
executive.cta_pattern: No explicit CTA. End with a thought, observation, or open ended question. The P.S. carries the action path.
executive.body_shape: Hook with reaction (1 to 2 sentences). One short paragraph showing you understand their world. Optional half thought. Sign off.
executive.ps_emphasis: Essential. The P.S. is the primary action path. Always offer a forward route if they are not the right person.
executive.aor_positioning: Implied or briefly stated. Do not lead with it. If included, frame as personal stake ('this is the work my team enjoys most'), not third party validation ('we get recommended').
avoid: Explaining what StreamAd does in detail

director.posture: Advisor to advisor. They have the budget and the number. Show them you understand the work and the stakes.
director.subject_style: Style 4 question form, One question on {their thing}? Specific to their situation, personal first. Never all lowercase.
subject_examples: One question on the campus split?
One question on your YouTube push?
director.cta_pattern: Open question they would actually want to answer. Hand them the floor. Never the generic 'Open to a quick conversation?' close.
director.body_shape: Hook with reaction (1 to 2 sentences). One paragraph framing the work with AOR positioning carrying personal stake. One open question. Sign off.
director.ps_emphasis: Recommended. Redirect if paid sits with someone else on their team.
director.aor_positioning: Direct and welcome. Vary construction across emails (this is the work my team does most, faith based is where we do our best stuff, we get recommended for mission driven orgs, etc.). Always tie to a specific peer stake.
avoid: Tactical jargon they would not use in conversation
Closing with the generic conversation ask

manager.posture: Collaborator to collaborator. They execute, they champion up. Frame yourself as a useful peer they can borrow brain power from.
manager.subject_style: Style 4 question form, One question on {their thing}? Tactical and easy to answer, never all lowercase.
subject_examples: One question on your paid mix?
One question on the campus geo split?
One question on the opening creative?
manager.cta_pattern: Tactical specific question they can answer in one line. Often channel or campaign scoped. Lower stakes than the director ask.
manager.body_shape: Hook with reaction (1 sentence). One paragraph offering practical observation or insight from similar work. One specific tactical question. Sign off.
manager.ps_emphasis: Optional. If used, mention the AOR conversation belongs at their director level if they want to bring you in.
manager.aor_positioning: Light touch. Frame as 'this is what we do for orgs like yours when it makes sense.' Door is open without feeling oversold.
avoid: Treating them as if they will buy directly

seniority_messaging_tiers.rule_for_mismatched_signals: If seniority enum disagrees with title text (e.g., seniority says 'director' but title says 'CMO'), follow the title text. Titles are more reliable than enum tags from Apollo.

personalization_sources.purpose: Tells the drafter where to pull personalization from inside each prospect record. Use these BEFORE falling back to generic industry language. Empty personalization is a signal to slow down, not paper over.

priority_order.use: Sets the tone and angle for the entire email. If present, treat as the brief for this prospect.
Pull one specific item for the hook (exhibition, program, campus, campaign, raise, hire). Name it AND react to it.
Drives timing framing. Why this prospect, right now. Often becomes the second paragraph anchor.
Funding, hiring, intent, tech gap. Supporting evidence not lead material. Use in body, not hook, unless the signal IS the news.
Informs angle. Heavy pixels without GA4 is a real observation. Empty site = conversation is about building.
Confirms which StreamAd service most likely matches. Shapes positioning, not copy.

Tone register modifier: churches more reverent + community minded, museums more cultural + audience minded, foundations more mission + donor minded, government more civic, commercial more outcome focused.

personalization_sources.fallback_behavior: If Look Deeper fields are thin or empty, surface the gap rather than generating a generic agency template. Empty personalization means stop and flag, do not paper over with 'we get recommended' filler.
personalization_sources.ai_messaging_decision: AI as a service is NOT mentioned in cold outreach (Phase 2 capability only). Mission driven and faith based prospects can be wary of AI in marketing language. AI shows up later in the relationship if at all. Internal AI usage by StreamAd (efficiency) is never mentioned to prospects.

icp.icp_name: StreamAd Direct Business Ideal Customer Profile
icp.strategy_note: ICP owned by Anna, set 2026-05-29. Three real tiers, tier_1 primary mission driven organizations, tier_2 secondary home services and high value local contractors, tier_3 financial institutions. Each sweep pulls a fixed mix per CFG.sweep_allocation (2 from each tier, total 6), with shortfalls backfilled from tier_1. Tier 1 band is marketing budget anchored ($100K+ marketing budget, not org revenue), so the Apollo revenue filter is a proxy and scoring plus seniority carry the quality, not the firmographic floor alone. Tier 2 home services uses Anna's $2M to $50M revenue band. Tier 3 financial qualifies by institution type plus sanity floors (20 to 500 employees, 5+ years), no revenue band per Drew. Intent signals ideal but not required, treat as scoring boost.

company.band_basis_note: Anna's band is annual marketing budget $100K and up, often $100K to $10M+. This range is an Apollo revenue proxy since Apollo cannot filter on marketing budget. Quality is carried by scoring and seniority, not this floor.

Anna's home services band, $2M to $50M+ revenue. High value local contractors, owner led, urgent buying cycles. Often overspending on Google search, the angle is perceived scale and authority via streaming.

company.qualification_basis: Type plus sanity floors, no revenue band (Drew, 2026-05-29). Qualify on the financial institution type list below plus 20 to 500 employees and 5+ years old. No annual_revenue_range_usd gate on this tier by design, a community bank or a wealth firm qualifies on what it is, not its revenue.

sweep_picker.notes: Optional quick-pick chips for the artifact's custom sweep picker, added 2026-05-31. These mirror the StreamAd Website 2.0 'Industries pillar hub' sub-verticals (Drew's emphasis list). Each chip maps a friendly label to one or more industries that already exist in the tier's industries_preferred list, so the Apollo search targets real values. The picker is OPTIONAL: a sweep with no chips checked for a tier pulls that tier's full industries_preferred list per the ICP. Checking chips narrows that tier's pull to only the selected sub-verticals. This block lives inside icp so sync-config.py serializes it into the artifact's DEFAULT_ICP automatically, no sync-script change needed. Single source of truth preserved.

must_have: Marketing communications development or outreach function ownership
Meets its tier gate, tier 1 mission driven $100K+ marketing budget proxy, tier 2 home services $2M to $50M revenue, tier 3 financial qualifies on institution type (no revenue band)
nice_to_have: Multi location or multi campus footprint
Mission driven or community oriented language

outreach_prioritization: Each sweep pulls a fixed mix per CFG.sweep_allocation (2 per tier). Ranking below orders candidates WITHIN each tier, it does not override the per tier quota.
Within a tier: fit + high intent, then fit + medium intent, then fit + affinity signal, then standard fit.
Tier priority for backfill when a tier underfills: tier_1 mission driven, then tier_2 home services, then tier_3 financial.
usage_notes: Intent boosts priority, never filters out
Budget and revenue treated as interchangeable
Affinity is a tiebreaker, not a filter
Prioritize the most senior comms or marketing contact per account on first pass
If Apollo intent is not available on plan, skip intent scoring and rank on firmographic and affinity fit only
Verified email is REQUIRED, skip any prospect without a confirmed deliverable email

pixel_taxonomy._notes: Canonical single source of truth for all paid pixels and tag management tags. Adding a new platform = one entry here, no other edits needed. SKILL.md STEP 4d uses detection_patterns to recognize tags from page HTML. The artifact reads label, is_core_chip, core_chip_order, and covered_aliases to render and dedupe pixel chips. SKILL.md Step 4g derives the independent DSP list from entries where is_independent_dsp is true. Schema per entry: label (display name), type ('paid' or 'tag_management'), detection_patterns (regex sources), is_core_chip (true = always rendered in the core chip row, false = only rendered in the additional row when detected), core_chip_order (sort order for core chips, low to high), covered_aliases (other taxonomy keys this chip absorbs to dedupe), is_independent_dsp (used by Step 4g for Fish-to-Dolphin promotion).

gtag.label: gtag
detection_patterns: googletagmanager.com/gtag/js with no AW or conversion id

paid_intensity_rules.notes: Computed in SWEEP_PROCEDURE.md Step 4g and stored on each ad_audit as paid_intensity. Drives the tier chip on the artifact card and the dropdown filter, and (as of 2026-06-01) is ALSO a SEARCH INPUT via the artifact sweep picker. Tier is a CLIENT-SIZE signal by EMPLOYEE COUNT ONLY, uniform across every industry (Drew, 2026-06-01). Revenue is NOT used to qualify size, it is still pulled from Apollo and shown on the card as a separate indicator. Bands: Fish 1 to 20 employees, Dolphin 21 to 100, Whale 101 to 1000. This replaced the old per-tier whale floors and the revenue/paid-activity promotion logic. Field name paid_intensity is kept for storage stability and to avoid touching HubSpot deal description JSON. The artifact carries a read-time translator that maps legacy heavy/light/none values from old prospects to whale/dolphin/fish on render.
paid_intensity_rules.classification_note: Classify a prospect's size from Apollo employee count, uniform for every industry: 1 to 20 = Fish, 21 to 100 = Dolphin, 101 and above = Whale. The 1000 in the whale band is the top Apollo bucket the picker pulls, not a hard ceiling on classification, a prospect over 1000 employees still classifies Whale. Employees only. No revenue gate, no paid-activity promotion. If Apollo has no employee count, default per data_missing_default.
paid_intensity_rules.known_whales_note: Optional manual override: a domain listed here is forced to Whale regardless of employee count. Kept as a safety hatch, it is not part of the automatic employee-band logic. Edit or empty as needed.

whale.label: Whale
whale.description: Big potential client, 101 to 1000 employees (or a domain in known_whales_domains). Brace for a full-tactics pitch across search, social, and programmatic.
whale.posture_implication: Big account, multi-tactic potential. Conversation is partnership and strategy at scale. Expect a long cycle and a meaningful contract, plan to invest the discovery and pitch effort accordingly.

dolphin.label: Dolphin
dolphin.description: Midsize org, 21 to 100 employees. Often one person wearing every marketing hat.
dolphin.posture_implication: Solid mid-tier opportunity. Conversation is improving execution quality and adding tactics they don't yet run. They value hands-on partnership.

fish.label: Fish
fish.description: Small org, 1 to 20 employees. Lean team, may need to scale workforce or budget before they are ready. Also covers prospects where Apollo employee data is missing entirely (flagged with data_status: missing on the audit).
fish.posture_implication: Lower-priority by contract ceiling. Conversation is whether paid belongs on the roadmap at all and whether they have the operational capacity to support it.
